scholarly.angle: Han Dynasty & Silk Road (206 BCE – 220 CE): sustained Han–Mediterranean contact existed but was almost entirely segmented intermediary trade (Parthian/Kushan/Sogdian middlemen), NOT direct Chinese–Roman exchange; Ferdinand von Richthofen's 1877 'Silk Road' framing projects 19th-century geographical imagination onto a network contemporaries experienced as disconnected regional circuits, and recent scholarship on the Indian Ocean maritime route may have been the larger East–West channel.
scholarly.key_claim: During the Han period, sustained commercial and diplomatic contact existed between Han China and the Mediterranean world — but this contact was almost entirely segmented intermediary trade through Parthian, Kushan, and Sogdian middlemen, NOT direct Chinese–Roman exchange; the modern unified 'Silk Road' framing (coined by Richthofen 1877) projects 19th-century geographical imagination onto a network that Han and Roman contemporaries experienced as disconnected regional circuits.

scholarly_debate.question: Is the Richthofen 'Silk Road' a useful heuristic for Han–Mediterranean contact, or a 19th-century historiographic projection that obscures the segmented, intermediary-driven reality (with maritime Indo-Roman trade likely the larger channel)?
scholarly_debate.position_a: The 'Silk Road' is a useful heuristic/umbrella concept: Hansen (2012) acknowledges it is a modern term but retains explanatory value for networks of regional exchange; Seland (2014), Franklin (2023), and Winter (2020/2022) treat 'Silk Roads' as an imperfect but workable framework for studying interregional connectivity.
scholarly_debate.position_b: The 'Silk Road' is a modern historiographic invention that distorts the ancient reality: Rezakhani (2010) argues it is a 19th-century name and invention that lumps individual histories together and creates long-distance connections where they never existed; Mishra (2020) and Darley (2024) show silk reached Rome primarily via India and the maritime route, and there was 'no direct contact between China and the Roman Empire'; Simmons (2023) proposes replacing 'Indo-Roman' with 'Indo-Mediterranean' to emphasize that trade was an intricate patchwork, not a unified channel.
scholarly_debate.current_consensus: Post-2010 scholarship substantially converges on the sharpened claim: Han–Mediterranean exchange was mediated by Parthian, Kushan, Sogdian, Palmyrene, Nabataean, and South Indian intermediaries; Hansen's point that no Roman coins dating to Han-contemporary centuries have been found in China is widely accepted (earliest Roman-style coins in China are Byzantine, 530s–540s CE). Recent Indian Ocean archaeology (Seland, Cobb, De Romanis) demonstrates the maritime route was at minimum comparable to and likely larger than the overland route for volume of East–West exchange.
scholarly_debate.resolution_needed: A synthetic global-history framework that (a) discards the Richthofen metageography as a unified route while preserving 'Silk Roads' (plural) as shorthand for multiple regional circuits, (b) integrates the Indian Ocean/maritime evidence on equal footing with overland Central Asian archaeology, and (c) acknowledges that Han and Roman contemporaries had almost no direct knowledge of each other — the Gan Ying episode (97 CE) being the partial exception that confirms the rule.

contested_claims.claim: Richthofen's 1877 'Silk Road' framing is a useful heuristic for ancient Eurasian exchange.
contested_claims.evidentiary_basis: Rezakhani (2010) explicitly argues the Silk Road 'is not only a nineteenth-century name but, indeed, a modern historiographical invention, serving to lump together individual histories and creating long-distance connections where they never existed'. Franklin (2023) traces how the concept was 'invented in the nineteenth century as an allegory for large scale human interaction across Eurasia' and surveys shared scalar challenges it creates. Sen (2023) and Winter (2020) show the concept has been further distorted by PRC Belt-and-Road instrumentalization. Mishra (2020) calls it 'anachronistic' and 'a backward projection of present into the historical past'. Kovács & Vér (2021) report that recent scholarship treats the Silk Roads as 'an intercontinental amalgam of numerous regional and interregional networks'. Against this, Hansen (2012, cited throughout the literature) retains the term with qualifications.

contested_claims.claim: Total volume of East–West trade was small (Raschke minimalism) vs. substantially larger than previously thought (McLaughlin / De Romanis revision).
contested_claims.evidentiary_basis: Seland (2014) summarizes that 'Raschke (1978) published his critical 700-page review … [and] found easy targets in conclusions that were inferred from meager sources and an archaeological record biased toward Mediterranean material'. Cobb (2015) demonstrates the peak intensity of Indo-Roman contact was in the late first century CE, with archaeological and numismatic evidence showing substantial but concentrated trade. De Romanis (2020) uses the Muziris papyrus to reconstruct a single Hermapollon cargo worth tens of millions of sesterces, demonstrating large-scale organized exchange. Darley (2024) reviews the post-2000 expansion of the field and its 'provincialisation' of Rome. The current balance supports upward revision from Raschke while accepting that the absolute numbers remain contested.

contested_claims.claim: The Gan Ying mission (97 CE) demonstrates meaningful Han–Roman diplomatic awareness.
contested_claims.evidentiary_basis: Selbitschka (2018) summarizes the scholarly consensus: 'the visitor was probably a private merchant masquerading as an official envoy … the Roman and Chinese courts remained distant from one another (Leslie and Gardiner 1996:161). The majority of exchanges between the Eastern Roman and Chinese cultural spheres were focused on commerce done in stages'. The episode is widely cited but generally as evidence of limited, not extensive, awareness.

contested_claims.claim: The Indian Ocean / maritime Spice Route was a larger channel of East–West exchange than the overland Silk Road.
contested_claims.evidentiary_basis: Seland (2014, 121 citing publications) argues 'Rome's trade with the East' labels capture only a fraction of Indian Ocean exchange and positions the maritime route as a primary hub of Old World exchange. Cobb (2015) provides chronological evidence of peak intensity in the late 1st c. CE. De Romanis (2020) reconstructs single-cargo pepper trade volumes (the Hermapollon) that dwarf plausible overland silk volumes. Simmons (2023) reframes the field as 'Indo-Mediterranean'. Mishra (2020) argues whatever silk reached Rome came via India, not via Central Asia. Darley (2024) reviews the 'provincialising Rome' turn in Indian Ocean scholarship. The consensus across these post-2010 sources supports upgrading the maritime channel to at least parity and likely primacy in volume.

contested_claims.claim: Archaeological finds (Roman coins in Xi'an, silk in Palmyra) indicate direct Han–Rome trade.
contested_claims.evidentiary_basis: Mishra (2020) cites Hansen directly: 'Had the trade between China and Rome been as significant as Pliny contended, some Roman coins would presumably have been found in China', and 'the earliest coins unearthed in China are from Byzantium, not Rome, and date to the 530s and 540s'. Selbitschka (2018) shows that Chinese jin-silks reaching Palmyra moved via pastoralist middlemen: 'jin-silks, in particular, were traded by pastoralists as middlemen'. The archaeological record is thus interpreted as long-chain diffusion through intermediaries (Xiongnu, Sogdian, Palmyrene merchants), not direct contact.

evidence.title: The Road That Never Was: The Silk Road and Trans-Eurasian Exchange
evidence.journal: Comparative Studies of South Asia, Africa and the Middle East
evidence.tier_rule_applied: humanities: citingPublicationsCount > 30 AND journal-article AND no editorial notices
evidence.key_finding: The central heterodox anchor of the debate. Rezakhani argues the Silk Road 'is not only a nineteenth-century name but, indeed, a modern historiographical invention, serving to lump together individual histories and creating long-distance connections where they never existed'. Proposes abandoning the concept for the sake of studying individual socioeconomic systems.
evidence.excerpt: The Silk Road is commonly used as a convenient blanket term to describe the many trade routes and points of contact that criss-crossed Central Asia. The term is generally overused, to the point that everything in the history of the region is conceptualized within the confines of the Silk Road(s).

evidence.title: Archaeology of Trade in the Western Indian Ocean, 300 BC–AD 700
evidence.tier_rule_applied: humanities: citingPublicationsCount > 30 AND journal-article AND no editorial notices
evidence.key_finding: Landmark synthesis making the maritime-primacy case. Seland argues that 'well-worn labels such as "Indo-Roman trade" or "Rome's trade with the East" allow us to grasp only a fraction of what ancient Indian Ocean exchange was about', and demonstrates that Berenike, Myos Hormos, Socotra, and Indian ports evidence a multi-polar cosmopolitan network far larger than the Rome-centric narrative admits. Explicitly positions Raschke's 1978 minimalism as based on 'meager sources and an archaeological record biased toward Mediterranean material'.
evidence.excerpt: When Raschke (1978) published his critical 700-page review of overland and maritime connections between Asia and the Roman Empire, he found easy targets in conclusions that were inferred from meager sources and an archaeological record biased toward Mediterranean material.

evidence.title: The Indo-Roman Pepper Trade and the Muziris Papyrus
evidence.tier_rule_applied: humanities: citingPublicationsCount > 30 (book monograph from OUP; no editorial notices)
evidence.key_finding: The revisionist scale-benchmark. The Muziris papyrus (P. Vindobonensis G 40822) documents a single cargo on the Hermapollon whose reconstruction by De Romanis demonstrates that Roman–South-Indian maritime trade was organized, heavily financed, and ran through customs regimes at Alexandria — evidence for volumes that undercut Raschke's minimalism and anchor the McLaughlin/Cobb revisionist position.
evidence.excerpt: Without these two texts, there would be no knowledge of the Indo-Roman trade practices … two texts become foundational resources for the history of commercial relationships between South India and the West.

evidence.title: The Chronology of Roman Trade in the Indian Ocean from Augustus to Early Third Century CE
evidence.journal: Journal of the Economic and Social History of the Orient
evidence.key_finding: Establishes that the most intense period of Indo-Roman maritime exchange was in the late first century CE — directly contemporaneous with the Han. Challenges both the Julio-Claudian-peak and the late-second-century-peak theses.
evidence.excerpt: The archaeological and numismatic evidence for Roman trade in the Indian Ocean from the Augustan annexation of Egypt up to the early third century CE shows that the most intense period of contact and exchange was in the late first century CE.

evidence.title: Behind gold for pepper: The players and the game of Indo-Mediterranean trade
evidence.tier_rule_applied: humanities: new paper, <10 citing publications; included for recency and substantive argument
evidence.key_finding: Representative of the current 'Indo-Mediterranean' reframe. Argues 'Indo-Roman' is a misnomer: 'no one Rome traded with one India; rather, exchange between them involved an intricate patchwork of individuals, trading ventures, and organized communities'. Supports the intermediary/segmented view.
evidence.excerpt: 'Indo-Mediterranean' stands as a more accurate label for the series of trade connections explored in this article; it emphasizes a convergence between the distinct maritime worlds of the Mediterranean and Indian Ocean, rather than a mismatch of political and geographical terminology.

evidence.title: Indian Ocean Trade in the First Millennium CE: Taking the Romans out of Indo-Roman?
evidence.tier_rule_applied: humanities: new paper (2024), <10 citing publications; flagship Journal of Roman Studies review article; included for recency
evidence.key_finding: Authoritative 2024 state-of-the-field review in JRS. Documents the post-2000 expansion of Indian Ocean scholarship, its global/decolonial turn, and explicit efforts to 'provincialise or decentre Rome' — i.e. to discard precisely the framing that Richthofen's Silk Road concept sustains.
evidence.excerpt: This article offers an overview of the study of Roman trade with the Western Indian Ocean … examines key developments in the field, including the changing scope of analysis … and specifically the effort to provincialise or decentre Rome in historical narratives.

evidence.title: The 'Silk Road': Historical Perspectives and Modern Constructions
evidence.tier_rule_applied: humanities: citingPublicationsCount > 10 AND journal-article AND no editorial notices
evidence.key_finding: Explicit critique of the Silk Road as 'colonial provenance' and 'backward projection'. Argues silk reached Rome via India (not via Central Asia) and quotes Hansen on the absence of Roman coins in Han-era China: 'the earliest coins unearthed in China are from Byzantium, not Rome, and date to the 530s and 540s'. Frames the India-China cotton-for-silk trade as largely erased by the Silk Road narrative.
evidence.excerpt: Thus, whatever amount of silk originating in China might have reached the Roman Empire did so through India and not via the 'Silk Road'; 'the Romans did not know of the silk's Chinese origins' because there was no direct contact between China and the Roman Empire.

evidence.title: Archaeology of the Silk Road: Challenges of Scale and Storytelling
evidence.tier_rule_applied: humanities: citingPublicationsCount < 10 (recent 2023); flagship Journal of Archaeological Research review; included for substantive weight
evidence.key_finding: Recent authoritative archaeological review. States the Silk Road was 'invented in the nineteenth century as an allegory for large scale human interaction across Eurasia' and traces 'shared challenges confronted by Silk Road archaeologists' arising from the metaphor — including scalar mismatches between the grand narrative and the local material record.
evidence.excerpt: Invented in the nineteenth century as an allegory for large scale human interaction across Eurasia, the idea of 'the Silk Road' continues to shape archaeological investigations of trade, travel, cultural exchange, and mobility in the region between the Near East and East Asia.

evidence.title: Inventing the 'Maritime Silk Road'
evidence.tier_rule_applied: humanities: citingPublicationsCount > 10 AND journal-article AND no editorial notices
evidence.key_finding: Shows how the 'Maritime Silk Road' was invented as a China-centric concept in the 1980s and weaponized via Belt and Road Initiative. References Rezakhani (2010) as the critical examination of the original Silk Road 'term and its relevance to the realities of trans-Eurasian exchanges' and Hansen (2012) as the recent re-examination — further evidence that both Rezakhani and Hansen anchor the current debate.
evidence.excerpt: On the use of these terms prior to Richthofen, see Chin (2013) and Mertens (2019). A critical examination of the term and its relevance to the 'realities' of trans-Eurasian exchanges is found in Rezakhani (2010). For recent re-examination of the 'Silk Road', see Hansen (2012) and Frankopan (2015).

evidence.title: Mongols and the Silk Roads: an Overview
evidence.tier_rule_applied: humanities: citingPublicationsCount < 10; included for direct confirmation of historiographic arc and for citing Rezakhani as the 'extreme end' of the revisionist position
evidence.key_finding: Confirms the mainstream framing: 'Richthofen named this sprawling web of channels Seidenstraßen "Silk Roads" … trade routes between China, Transoxiana and India in the period of the Han dynasty'. Documents how 'the Silk Roads have recently come to be regarded rather as an intercontinental amalgam of numerous regional and interregional networks', citing Hansen (2017) on local perspectives and Rezakhani (2010) as the 'extreme end' of that approach.
evidence.excerpt: Hoppál 2020) and thus the Silk Roads have recently come to be regarded rather as an intercontinental amalgam of numerous regional and interregional networks. Consequently, scholars have stressed the significance of local perspectives (Hitch 2009; Vaissière 2014) and the importance of local trade (Hansen 2017). An extreme end of this approach questioned the validity of the Silk Roads concept (Rezakhani 2010).

evidence.title: Genuine Prestige Goods in Mortuary Contexts: Emulation in Polychrome Silk and Byzantine Solidi from Northern China
evidence.tier_rule_applied: humanities: citingPublicationsCount < 10; included because it directly cites Leslie & Gardiner (1996) on Gan Ying / Han–Roman diplomatic contact and provides direct evidence on intermediary silk trade
evidence.key_finding: Documents that 'the Roman and Chinese courts remained distant from one another' (citing Leslie & Gardiner 1996) and that jin-silks reached Palmyra via pastoralist middlemen — not via direct Han–Roman contact. Important anchor for contested_claims[3] (Gan Ying) and contested_claims[5] (archaeological finds as diffusion not direct trade).
evidence.excerpt: the visitor was probably a private merchant masquerading as an official envoy—and the establishment of a few embassies during the seventh century CE, the Roman and Chinese courts remained distant from one another (Leslie and Gardiner 1996:161). The majority of exchanges between the Eastern Roman and Chinese cultural spheres were focused on commerce done in stages.

gaps_observed: Valerie Hansen's The Silk Road: A New History (Oxford University Press, 2012) did not surface directly by DOI in Scite — only her journal articles (on Turfan textiles-as-money, Tang tax textiles, etc.) appeared. However, she is cited as the key recent re-examination anchor in Sen (2023), Mishra (2020) via direct quotations, and Kovács & Vér (2021). The synthesis-anchor function of Hansen (2012) is thus confirmed via secondary citation even though the monograph is not indexed with a Scite DOI that matched my searches.

Xinru Liu's The Silk Road in World History (2010) and Ancient India and Ancient China (1988) did not surface directly by DOI. This is a Scite indexing gap (older and non-journal monographs are poorly indexed).

Manfred Raschke's 1978 'New Studies in Roman Commerce with the East' did not surface directly — it is pre-DOI era — but is cited prominently in Seland (2014) and is clearly treated as the minimalist baseline that McLaughlin/De Romanis/Cobb revise upward.

Raoul McLaughlin's The Roman Empire and the Indian Ocean (2014) did not surface directly in these searches; De Romanis (2020) and Cobb (2015) carry equivalent revisionist weight in the evidence register.
Roberta Tomber's Indo-Roman Trade: From Pots to Pepper (2008) did not surface directly but is cited in Seland (2014) as a core reference for ceramic evidence from Berenike.
Leslie & Gardiner's The Roman Empire in Chinese Sources (1996) did not surface directly (pre-DOI monograph) but is cited in Selbitschka (2018) as the authority on courts remaining distant.
No retractions, corrections, or expressions of concern were found on any of the 42 papers retrieved. This is expected for humanities historiography but worth noting explicitly.

Direct archaeological papers on Han-dynasty Roman coin finds (e.g. the disputed Xi'an aureus) did not surface in Scite — the field's coin-chronology debate is carried in numismatic-specialist venues not well-indexed here.
